expectations.text: Output file is a duplicate (not the original) — original 02-portfolio-minimal.html is untouched
expectations.evidence: Output is 02-portfolio-minimal-animated.html (466 lines with GSAP scripts and animations). Original 02-portfolio-minimal.html remains at 98 lines with no GSAP code, original CSS transitions intact, no data-magnetic attributes — fully untouched.

expectations.text: GSAP core + ScrollTrigger CDN scripts are included via script tags
SplitText plugin is loaded from cdn.jsdelivr.net and registered
Hero has a GSAP timeline with 4+ chained .fromTo or .from calls
expectations.evidence: The hero timeline (heroTl) only has 2 chained calls: (1) heroTl.from(split.words, {...}) at line 153 and (2) heroTl.fromTo(".hero .subtitle", ...) at line 162. This falls short of the required 4+. The transcript self-verify claims PASS but miscounts — SplitText word stagger is a single .from() call, not multiple.

expectations.text: gsap.matchMedia wraps animation code with prefers-reduced-motion guard
expectations.evidence: Line 130: const mm = gsap.matchMedia(). Line 135: mm.add("(prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference)", () => { ... }) wraps all animation code. Line 444: mm.add("(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)", () => { ... }) provides the reduced-motion fallback with clearProps.

expectations.text: autoAlpha is used instead of bare opacity in gsap.from/fromTo calls
expectations.evidence: All gsap.from/fromTo calls use autoAlpha (20+ occurrences across lines 155, 163-164, 205-207, 232-234, 279-281, 295-297, 316-318, 335-337, 349-351, 366-368, 423, 426). The only bare 'opacity' occurrences are in CSS (nav links initial styling and cursor-ring initial styling), not in GSAP calls.

expectations.text: ScrollTrigger scroll reveals cover projects, about, and contact sections
expectations.evidence: Projects: scrollTrigger on .project-item elements (line 237) and [data-scramble] Selected Works heading (line 216). About: scrollTrigger on .about-label (line 284), .about-text (line 300), and .skills-list (line 322). Contact: scrollTrigger on .contact (line 340) and .contact-email (line 354).

expectations.text: At least one interactive effect exists (magnetic, spring hover, or tilt)
expectations.evidence: Three interactive effects present: (1) Magnetic cursor pull on [data-magnetic] elements (line 384-395, Pattern 3), (2) Spring hover on project items with back.out(1.7) easing (lines 250-267, Pattern 7), (3) Custom cursor follower with ring scaling on hoverable elements (lines 402-430, Pattern 14).

expectations.text: No CSS transition conflicts with GSAP-owned properties
expectations.evidence: Zero active CSS 'transition:' declarations in the output file. All original transitions were removed with explanatory comments: nav links (line 18), project-title (line 30), skills-list li (line 40), contact-email (line 45), footer a (line 49).

expectations.text: Token comments are present (e.g. // token: base, // token: entrance)
expectations.evidence: 26+ token comments throughout: // token: impact (lines 149, 157, 236, 283, 339, 353, 386), // token: slow (lines 156, 298), // token: fast (lines 188, 191, 255, 262), // token: entrance (lines 164, 263, 299, 320, 370, 426), // token: spring (lines 256, 423), // token: epic (line 208), // token: micro (line 410), and compound forms like // token: fast / impact.

expectations.text: gsap.registerPlugin() is called for all used plugins
expectations.evidence: Line 127: gsap.registerPlugin(ScrollTrigger, SplitText, ScrambleTextPlugin). All three plugins loaded via CDN (ScrollTrigger, SplitText, ScrambleTextPlugin) are registered. All three are actually used in the code.

expectations.text: ScrollTrigger.refresh() is called on window load event

claims.claim: Hero timeline with 4+ chained calls — FAIL
claims.evidence: The corrected transcript records FAIL, and only 2 chained calls exist on heroTl (.from on words + .fromTo on subtitle). The SplitText stagger counts as one call, not multiple.

claims.claim: 12 animation patterns applied across the page
claims.evidence: Metrics.json lists 12 patterns. Code inspection confirms all 12 are implemented: SplitText hero, hero subtitle, smart navbar, scramble text, project stagger reveal, spring hover, about labels, about text, skills list, contact reveal, footer reveal, magnetic cursor, custom cursor.

claims.claim: 5 CSS transitions removed to prevent conflicts
claims.evidence: 5 CSS transition removal comments found (nav links, project-title, skills-list li, contact-email, footer a), and metrics.json now reports 5 as well.

claims.claim: Personality: dramatic with slow-epic duration bias
claims.evidence: Token comments confirm dramatic personality: durations range 0.8-1.5s for reveals (slow-epic), impact easing (power3.out) used frequently, stagger values in loose range (0.06-0.07).
Line 129: const isTouch = 'ontouchstart' in window || navigator.maxTouchPoints > 0. Lines 383 and 402: if (!isTouch) wraps magnetic pull and custom cursor.

claims.claim: Cleanup function returned from matchMedia callback
claims.evidence: Lines 432-438: return () => { heroTl.kill(); splitInstance.revert(); navST.kill(); ScrollTrigger.getAll().forEach(t => t.kill()); }

suggestions.assertion: Hero has a GSAP timeline with 4+ chained .fromTo or .from calls
suggestions.reason: This expectation is well-defined and discriminating — it caught a genuine shortfall. The skill's self-verify incorrectly claimed PASS. Consider clarifying whether staggered calls on arrays of elements count as 1 call or N calls, as this ambiguity could cause inconsistent grading.
No assertion checks whether SplitText uses accessibility features (aria: true) or responsive re-splitting (autoSplit: true). These are quality differentiators the skill produced but go unmeasured.

No assertion validates that the reduced-motion fallback actually makes all elements visible (clearProps: 'all'). A broken fallback would leave elements invisible for users who prefer reduced motion — a significant accessibility failure that currently goes untested.

eval_feedback.overall: Strong assertion set that covers the core GSAP quality signals. The hero timeline assertion correctly caught a real shortcoming. Adding a reduced-motion fallback check and an accessibility assertion would round out the coverage.
